thinkingSummary: The population reconciliation shows 334 accounts total with no discrepancies, 332 in-scope after excluding service accounts, and decisions made to retain 331 and revoke 1, with 2 flagged as out of scope—all reviewed by Priya Nadkarni on 2026-06-30. Now I need to verify whether Priya herself appears in the reviewed accounts and cross-check against HRIS data to identify any terminated employees who still have active access, which the review has already surfaced at least one instance of.

thinkingSummary:
The reperformance uncovered a critical discrepancy: while the original review flagged only Danielle Goodwin as a terminated employee with active access, my analysis found a second person—Kevin Lewis, terminated in July 2021—who also retained NetSuite access but wasn't caught. The "On Leave" workers check out fine since they're legitimately supposed to be active.

I also notice Priya Nadkarni, who approved the review, doesn't appear in the HRIS export at all and has no NetSuite account, which raises questions about verifying her authority through HRIS. The cover documentation identifies her as Director of Finance Systems, but I need to search HRIS more thoroughly to see if she's listed under a different email format or name variation. Let me pull Kevin Lewis's original review decision and comments, then do a broader search for Nadkarni in the system.

input.goal: Check Kevin Lewis review decision, and search HRIS for the reviewer Priya Nadkarni under any email/name variant

goal: Check Kevin Lewis review decision, and search HRIS for the reviewer Priya Nadkarni under any email/name variant

kevin.Name: Kevin Lewis
kevin.Reviewer Comment: Confirmed with line manager - access still required.

thinkingSummary:
I've found 14 terminated HRIS employees with NetSuite accounts in the export, but I need to verify which ones actually have active account status versus those already deprovisioned. Looking at the data, only Danielle Goodwin and Kevin Lewis show up as both terminated and actively provisioned in NetSuite.

The other 12 terminated employees have inactive accounts, which aligns with the 12 inactive accounts in the status counts — so those are properly deprovisioned. But Kevin Lewis is the critical issue here: he was terminated on 2021-07-26 yet still retains active NetSuite access, and unlike Danielle Goodwin (who was flagged for revocation), Kevin's access was explicitly retained with a note that his line manager confirmed he still needs it. This is a missed exception — the reviewer approved access for a terminated employee, which represents both a control failure in identifying inappropriate access and a remediation failure since the access should have been revoked upon termination.

Now I'm mapping this to the control attributes. A1 is clearly met — the review was quarterly and completed on schedule. For A2, the mechanical requirement is satisfied: Priya Nadkarni reviewed and approved all 334 accounts as the designated System Owner, and there's no self-review conflict since she has no account herself. However, I can't independently verify her authority through the HRIS export since she doesn't appear in it, though the cover letter designates her as System Owner. The real issue is whether A2 should account for review accuracy — Kevin Lewis's account was reviewed and approved, but the decision to retain access for a terminated employee was wrong. That accuracy failure actually belongs to A3, which is about identifying and remediating inappropriate access. Kevin's account is inappropriate access that wasn't identified during the review, so A3 fails.

For Danielle Goodwin, the control did identify her excessive access and raised a ticket within the SLA window, but the export shows her account still Active as of the review date, so remediation isn't yet evidenced as complete. That's a partial failure on timeliness. The core issue is Kevin Lewis — terminated in 2021, still Active, marked Retain during review, and never remediated. That's a clear control failure on A3. document both findings clearly — Danielle's ticket is raised but not confirmed complete, and Kevin Lewis was retained despite termination, which is a clear control failure. The reperformance contradicts the recorded conclusion that claimed only one exception, so this is a deviation. I'm verifying the account statuses in the system (both show Active), checking that the 12 inactive accounts map to terminated employees, and confirming no deprovisioning evidence exists beyond the ticket for Danielle.

input.goal: Get SAE records for Danielle Goodwin and Kevin Lewis, and confirm the 12 inactive accounts all map to terminated HRIS workers

goal: Get SAE records for Danielle Goodwin and Kevin Lewis, and confirm the 12 inactive accounts all map to terminated HRIS workers

thinkingSummary:
I've confirmed that 12 inactive accounts properly correspond to terminated workers, but found a critical issue: Kevin Lewis has an active account despite being terminated in July 2021, and his account shows a last login in June 2026—well after his termination date, indicating he's still actively accessing the system. Danielle Goodwin was already flagged, but Kevin Lewis was missed and marked to retain.

This is a clear failure for A3. The reviewer's note claiming "access still required" for a terminated employee directly contradicts what HRIS shows.

For A2, all 334 records were reviewed and approved by Priya Nadkarni as System Owner, which is appropriate for a NetSuite access review. However, the review was demonstrably inaccurate given the Kevin Lewis miss. I'm rating A2 as SUCCESS but noting that the effectiveness concern is captured under A3's failure. Now I'll document the evidence and submit.

attributes.summary: The NetSuite Q2 2026 access review was performed on a quarterly cadence and all decisions are dated 2026-06-30, within the Q2 2026 period.
attributes.reasoning: The Cover identifies the engagement as a 'Periodic (Quarterly) User Access Review' for NetSuite (Production), Review Period Q2 2026, with Review Completed 2026-06-30 and an electronic sign-off dated 2026-06-30 [E2 sheet:Cover]. All 334 Access Review records carry a single Review Date of 2026-06-30, which falls within the Q2 2026 quarter (Apr-Jun 2026) [Q1]. Both the NetSuite access source and the independent Workday roster were exported 26 Jun 2026 [E2 sheet:Cover!B9-B10], consistent with a quarter-end review. The control does not fix an exact cadence beyond 'periodic (e.g. quarterly)'; a dated Q2 review completed at quarter-end satisfies the periodicity requirement.

evidence.observation: Review Period = Q2 2026; Review Type = Periodic (Quarterly) User Access Review; Review Completed 2026-06-30; sign-off 2026-06-30.
All 334 Access Review records have Review Date 2026-06-30.

attributes.summary: Every in-scope account was reviewed and a decision recorded, and the review was approved by the designated NetSuite system owner (Priya Nadkarni, Director, Finance Systems); no self-review occurred.
attributes.reasoning: All 334 accounts have a populated Reviewer Decision and a named reviewer with no blanks [Q1]. The sole reviewer/approver is Priya Nadkarni, designated on the Cover as 'Director, Finance Systems (System Owner)', with an electronic approval sign-off dated 2026-06-30 [E2 sheet:Cover]. A NetSuite system access review approved by the NetSuite system owner is an appropriate owner for the system/data in scope. No self-review risk exists: Priya Nadkarni holds no NetSuite account in the export [Q3]. I could not independently corroborate her title/authority because she does not appear in the HRIS export [Q3]; the appropriateness rests on the Cover's designation (a management representation). Given the system-owner designation and the absence of a self-review conflict, the attribute is met, though the inability to corroborate the reviewer's role via an independent source lowers confidence and is noted as an observation. Note: the reviewer's decisions were not uniformly accurate (see A3), but that is a remediation/effectiveness matter rather than a question of whether an appropriate owner performed and approved the review.

evidence.observation: All 334 records have a populated Reviewer Decision and Reviewed By = Priya Nadkarni; no blanks.
Reviewer/Approver = Priya Nadkarni, Director, Finance Systems (System Owner); approved electronically 2026-06-30.
Priya Nadkarni holds no NetSuite account (no self-review) and does not appear in the HRIS export.

attributes.summary: Independent reperformance found 2 terminated employees retaining ACTIVE NetSuite access, but the review identified and remediated only 1; a terminated user (Kevin Lewis) was affirmatively marked 'Retain' and his access was neither identified nor remediated.
attributes.reasoning: The control requires inappropriate/excessive access identified in the review to be remediated in a timely manner, and the review's stated objective was to reconcile access to the Workday roster. Reperforming that reconciliation over the full in-scope population, I joined all in-scope NetSuite accounts to the HRIS export and found TWO terminated workers with ACTIVE NetSuite access [Q3]: Danielle Goodwin (terminated 2021-12-03) and Kevin Lewis (terminated 2021-07-26). The review only flagged one. Danielle Goodwin was correctly identified, marked 'Revoke', and remediation initiated via ticket ITSM-48217 (due within 5 business days) [E2 sheet:Summary & Observations!B10-B12], though her account remained Active in the source export with a last login of 2026-06-14 [Q5], so completion of deprovisioning is not yet evidenced. Kevin Lewis, however, was marked 'Retain' with the comment 'Confirmed with line manager - access still required.' [Q4], directly contradicting HRIS which shows him terminated 2021-07-26; his account is still Active with a last login of 2026-06-04 [Q5]. This is inappropriate access to a production financial system by a terminated individual that the review failed to identify and did not remediate. The Summary's assertion that 'All other in-scope accounts reconciled to active/on-leave workers with no exceptions' and the conclusion of 'one noted exception' [E2 sheet:Summary & Observations!B10-B12] are contradicted by my reperformance. Because a recorded conclusion is contradicted by reperformance and inappropriate access exists that was not remediated, this is a deviation. (The 12 Inactive accounts all correctly map to terminated workers, confirming deprovisioning worked for those [Q5]; the 8 'On Leave' active accounts are legitimate and not exceptions.)

evidence.observation: Two Terminated HRIS workers retain ACTIVE NetSuite access: Danielle Goodwin (term 2021-12-03) and Kevin Lewis (term 2021-07-26).
Kevin Lewis (A/P Clerk, terminated 2021-07-26) received Reviewer Decision 'Retain' with comment 'Confirmed with line manager - access still required.'
Kevin Lewis account Active, last login 2026-06-04; Danielle Goodwin account Active, last login 2026-06-14; all 12 Inactive accounts map to Terminated workers.
Only Danielle Goodwin flagged; states all other accounts reconciled with no exceptions; conclusion 'Control operating with one noted exception.'

exceptions.title: Terminated employee retains active access, not identified or remediated
exceptions.detail: Kevin Lewis (kevin.lewis@northpeakfinancial.com, A/P Clerk) was terminated in HRIS on 2021-07-26 yet retains ACTIVE NetSuite (Production) access with a last login of 2026-06-04 [Q3][Q4][Q5]. The reviewer marked the account 'Retain' with the comment 'Confirmed with line manager - access still required.' [Q4], which is contradicted by the independent Workday roster. This inappropriate access (~4.9 years post-termination) was neither identified during the review nor remediated, and no remediation ticket exists for it, undermining the control's purpose of ensuring only authorised individuals retain access.

evidence.observation: Kevin Lewis marked 'Retain' despite HRIS termination 2021-07-26.
Kevin Lewis account Active, last login 2026-06-04.

exceptions.title: Identified revocation not evidenced as completed
exceptions.detail: Danielle Goodwin (terminated 2021-12-03) was correctly flagged for revocation with ticket ITSM-48217 raised (due within 5 business days) [E2 sheet:Summary & Observations!B10-B12], but her NetSuite account is still Active in the source export with a last login of 2026-06-14 [Q5]. Remediation was initiated but its completion is not evidenced; the only support is a raised ticket, a self-reported assertion. Timeliness is also poor given access persisted ~4.5 years after termination before detection.

evidence.observation: Danielle Goodwin flagged; ticket ITSM-48217 raised, due within 5 business days.
Danielle Goodwin account still Active, last login 2026-06-14.

reperformance.procedure: Independently reconciled all in-scope NetSuite accounts (334, less 2 out-of-scope service accounts) against the HRIS/Workday export by email to identify terminated or otherwise non-active workers retaining active NetSuite access.
reperformance.recordedResult: Review recorded 1 exception: only Danielle Goodwin identified as terminated-but-active; 'All other in-scope accounts reconciled to active/on-leave workers with no exceptions.'
reperformance.reperformedResult: Found 2 terminated workers with ACTIVE NetSuite access: Danielle Goodwin (term 2021-12-03) and Kevin Lewis (term 2021-07-26). Kevin Lewis was marked 'Retain' and not flagged.
reperformance.detail: The review missed one terminated-but-active account. Kevin Lewis [Q3][Q4] is terminated per HRIS (2021-07-26) yet Active in NetSuite with last login 2026-06-04 [Q5], and was incorrectly retained. The 12 Inactive accounts all correctly map to terminated workers and the 8 On-Leave active accounts are legitimate [Q3][Q5], so the sole delta is Kevin Lewis.

reperformance.recordedResult: 332 in-scope accounts reviewed (331 Retain, 1 Revoke), 2 service accounts out of scope, reviewed by Priya Nadkarni, completed 2026-06-30.
reperformance.reperformedResult: Confirmed: 334 records each fully populated (331 Retain, 1 Revoke, 2 N/A-Out of Scope), all reviewed by Priya Nadkarni, all dated 2026-06-30; populations reconcile fully with no orphan accounts.
reperformance.detail: Decision completeness, reviewer, dating, and population counts agree with the workpaper [Q1][Q2].

observations.title: Reviewer's authority could not be independently corroborated
observations.detail: The reviewer/approver Priya Nadkarni is designated on the Cover as Director, Finance Systems (System Owner) [E2 sheet:Cover] but does not appear in the HRIS/Workday export [Q3], so her role and employment could not be independently corroborated. Appropriateness of the approver rests solely on the workpaper's self-designation.

evidence.observation: Reviewer/Approver = Priya Nadkarni, Director, Finance Systems (System Owner).
Priya Nadkarni does not appear in the HRIS export.

observations.title: Terminated users continued logging into NetSuite post-termination
observations.detail: Both terminated-but-active accounts show recent logins (Kevin Lewis 2026-06-04; Danielle Goodwin 2026-06-14) [Q5], indicating live use of production financial system access years after termination (2021-07-26 and 2021-12-03 respectively), which elevates the risk from these access gaps.

evidence.observation: Kevin Lewis last login 2026-06-04; Danielle Goodwin last login 2026-06-14.

input.conclusion: The Q2 2026 NetSuite user access review was performed on a quarterly basis and completed 2026-06-30 (A1 SUCCESS), and every in-scope account was reviewed and approved by the designated NetSuite system owner with no self-review (A2 SUCCESS, medium confidence as the reviewer could not be corroborated in HRIS). However, remediation of inappropriate access fails (A3 FAIL): independent reconciliation to the Workday roster identified two terminated employees retaining active access, but the review flagged only one; Kevin Lewis (terminated 2021-07-26) was incorrectly marked 'Retain' and his access was neither identified nor remediated, contradicting the workpaper's 'no other exceptions' conclusion. The one identified item (Danielle Goodwin) has a remediation ticket but no evidence of completed deprovisioning. The control is not operating effectively for the remediation attribute.

thinkingSummary:
Looking at the assessment results, the first two claims check out: all 334 entries are dated 2026-06-30 in Q2 as confirmed, and the reviewer field is consistently populated with just Priya Nadkarni handling everything with no self-reviews. I need to verify whether Priya actually exists in the HRIS system and if she has a NetSuite account. The third claim appears to be failing—there are only 2 records showing as terminated but still active.

Regarding the inactive accounts, they all map correctly to terminated workers, confirming the deprovisioning process worked. Though the reviewer marked them "Retain," the accounts are already deprovisioned so there's no active access issue, even if the decision seems odd. The key finding remains that A3 is failing with those 2 terminated-active accounts confirmed. I should also verify Priya's HRIS status and check whether the "activeNotInHris" group really contains only the 2 service accounts as claimed. Let me look into the second exception mentioned in the assessment regarding Danielle Goodwin. A2 hinges on whether Priya qualifies as an appropriate owner—the workpaper self-designates her as System Owner, and the assessment acknowledges this limitation by marking it medium confidence and treating it as an observation rather than a control failure, which seems reasonable. A1's dates all align to 2026-06-30 as the quarter-end cutoff, so that's solid. Let me verify whether Priya actually appears in the HRIS and NetSuite exports to confirm the assessment's factual claims.

thinkingSummary:
I've verified Priya's absence from both HRIS and the NetSuite export, with eight active on-leave records confirming the data. The A3 failure and its supporting exceptions are solid, so now I'm checking whether the assessment overstates anything—particularly around exception #2 for Danielle Goodwin regarding the remediation claim.

The key issue is timing: the System Access Export was pulled on 26 June 2026, but the review wasn't completed until 30 June 2026, and the remediation ticket was raised as part of that review. So the account showing as Active in the export actually predates the remediation process entirely—the export can't evidence incomplete remediation when it was taken before remediation was even initiated. This is a logical flaw in how the exception is framed, though the broader point that there's no positive evidence of completion still holds. It's a minor overstatement that doesn't undermine the FAIL verdict, which stands firmly on Kevin Lewis anyway. I should note that the export timing creates a slight imprecision in the framing—the account being Active in the pre-review export doesn't actually evidence non-completion since the export predates the ticket. But the assessment's core claim that completion isn't evidenced is still accurate, and the A3 FAIL verdict remains solid on Kevin Lewis alone. For the twelve inactive accounts marked "Retain," they're correctly inactive so there's no inappropriate access issue, which means they don't constitute an exception to the control. Let me verify the scope count: 332 in-scope accounts should map to 331 retain plus 1 revoke, with 2 out-of-scope service accounts for a total of 334. The export timing issue with Danielle Goodwin is a minor logical imprecision—the "so" in the reasoning suggests the active status proves non-completion, but really the export just can't show completion since it predates the ticket. The conclusion itself (completion not evidenced) is accurate though, so it doesn't undermine the assessment. All three verdicts hold up as sound. For Danielle, the export from 26 June predates the review completion on 30 June, so her Active status in that export doesn't actually evidence a remediation failure — the ticket was appropriately raised within SLA. This is a legitimate note worth flagging: exception #2's foundation is weaker than it appears, though it doesn't change the overall FAIL verdict since that rests on Kevin Lewis's unticket remediation.

I'm thinking through how to structure this in the challenge framework — whether I can confirm A3's verdict while raising a note about the secondary exception's reasoning, since the verdict itself still stands even if one supporting exception is analytically flawed. The note on exception #2 is worth including since it flags that the deviation isn't actually proven—listing it without evidence would overstate the findings. So I'm settling on: A1 and A2 confirmed, A3 confirmed as FAIL with a note on exception #2, and I'll write it up now.
text:
My re-derivation confirms all the load-bearing facts:

- **A1**: R1 shows all 334 Access Review records dated 2026-06-30, within Q2 2026; Cover confirms period/type/completion. SUCCESS stands.
- **A2**: R1 confirms 0 blank decisions/reviewers, single reviewer "Priya Nadkarni" for all 334, decisions {retain:331, revoke:1, N/A-out-of-scope:2}. R4 confirms Priya appears in neither HRIS nor the NetSuite export (no self-review), so appropriateness rests on the Cover designation — appropriately hedged to medium confidence. SUCCESS stands.
- **A3**: R2 confirms populations fully reconcile (334=334, no orphans, 322 active/12 inactive). R3 confirms exactly 2 terminated-but-ACTIVE workers — Danielle Goodwin (Revoke) and Kevin Lewis (Retain, term 2021-07-26, last login 2026-06-04) — plus 2 service accounts (out of scope), and all 12 inactive accounts map to terminated workers. Kevin Lewis was affirmatively retained and not remediated. FAIL stands.

One analytical imprecision in A3's secondary exception is worth recording. The NetSuite export was pulled **26 Jun 2026** (Cover B9), four days *before* the review was completed and the Danielle Goodwin remediation ticket was raised (**2026-06-30**, Cover B13 / Summary). So her account still showing "Active" in that export cannot evidence a remediation failure — the export predates the remediation. This does not affect the FAIL verdict, which rests independently on Kevin Lewis.

challenges.challenge: The A3 FAIL verdict is sound and rests firmly on Kevin Lewis (terminated 2021-07-26, marked 'Retain', account Active with last login 2026-06-04, not identified or remediated). However, the secondary exception concerning Danielle Goodwin overstates the evidence for a remediation-completion/timeliness deviation. The NetSuite access export was pulled 26 Jun 2026, whereas the review was completed and remediation ticket ITSM-48217 was raised on 30 Jun 2026 — the export predates the ticket by four days. Danielle Goodwin's account therefore necessarily still shows 'Active' in that export, so the observation 'still Active in the source export ... so completion of deprovisioning is not yet evidenced' cannot demonstrate a failed or untimely remediation; the export simply cannot reflect any post-review deprovisioning. For the identified item, remediation was ticketed with a 5-business-day SLA and no breach is evidenced, so no proven remediation-timeliness deviation exists for Danielle Goodwin. The '~4.5 years' framing also conflates the historical access gap with remediation timeliness (which runs from identification). The verdict survives on Kevin Lewis regardless.

evidence.observation: Source of Access = 'NetSuite > Setup > Users/Roles export (26 Jun 2026)' (B9); Review Completed = 2026-06-30 (B13).
Danielle Goodwin flagged for immediate revocation; deprovisioning ticket ITSM-48217 raised, due within 5 business days (B10).

Danielle Goodwin appears Active with last login 2026-06-14 in the export — an export dated 26 Jun 2026, i.e. before the 30 Jun review completion and ticket, so it cannot evidence non-completion of remediation.

input.summary: All three verdicts withstand review. A1 (SUCCESS) and A2 (SUCCESS) are fully corroborated by independent re-derivation (complete, quarter-end-dated decisions; single designated system owner; no self-review; reviewer appropriately not corroborable in HRIS). A3 (FAIL) is correct and well-supported: reperformance confirms two terminated-but-active accounts where the review flagged only one, with Kevin Lewis affirmatively retained. The sole recorded item is a note: A3's secondary exception on Danielle Goodwin infers a remediation failure from an export (26 Jun) that predates the review/ticket (30 Jun), which the evidence does not support — this does not disturb the FAIL verdict.

review.summary: All three verdicts withstand review. A1 (SUCCESS) and A2 (SUCCESS) are fully corroborated by independent re-derivation (complete, quarter-end-dated decisions; single designated system owner; no self-review; reviewer appropriately not corroborable in HRIS). A3 (FAIL) is correct and well-supported: reperformance confirms two terminated-but-active accounts where the review flagged only one, with Kevin Lewis affirmatively retained. The sole recorded item is a note: A3's secondary exception on Danielle Goodwin infers a remediation failure from an export (26 Jun) that predates the review/ticket (30 Jun), which the evidence does not support — this does not disturb the FAIL verdict.
